story:
Detective Elijah Crain was Doverton’s hero—the Watcher. He chased what the city feared. Then autumn came hard. Little girls vanished from the south side; bodies surfaced along the river. At a press conference the department made Crain promise justice. The city believed him.

Sergeant Dana Rowen had worked with him before. Smart, relentless, odd. She muttered into a battered recorder, chewed hard candy, and handed out butterscotch like calling cards. It endeared her to some. It grated on Crain.

The new case: Emma Darcy, sixteen, weekend waitress at the Dover Diner, hair ribbon, silver locket. She didn’t come home. Crain stared at her photograph and swallowed his dread. Rowen clicked her recorder and spoke for the invisible file—age, last seen, probable abduction—then unwrapped another candy. She offered him one. He waved it off.

They canvassed the diner, the school, the street. No one saw anything. The pressure tightened. Three nights later Emma turned up in an abandoned lot by the river, blue-faced, ribbon loose.

At the morgue, the coroner worked carefully. Cause of death: airway obstructed. He tugged something free with forceps and let it fall to the tray. A butterscotch. Half-dissolved. Crain’s gaze slid to Rowen. She kept sucking a candy, unbothered. The coroner called it odd but not impossible. Crain could not look away.

In the hallway, Martin Nelson, the night guard—quiet man, late forties, keys like a rosary—told Crain he looked terrible. He spoke softly about kids who shouldn’t die. He mused that candy could be forced into a mouth. And, almost idly, he noted that Rowen gave him a sweet every night. The seed lodged.

Four days later, Leah Mercer vanished. No body. No trace. Rowen kept working—scribbling theories, recording, offering candy to rookies with shaking hands. Into her recorder she spoke in a low, certain murmur: “He whistles as he unlocks his basement. The door swings open.” Crain watched her chew, watched foil unwrap with ritual care, imagined Leah choking in the dark on a butterscotch she hadn’t wanted. Suspicion turned fever.

Weeks passed. Another girl washed up on the riverbank. Crain snapped. He barreled under the tape, suit rumpled, tie askew, and dropped to his knees in the mud. He pried the dead girl’s jaw wide, nails scraping gums, screaming about candy, about Rowen, about proof lodged in a throat. Cameras caught everything. Rowen stood pale and silent, recorder in hand. “He’s desecrating the body,” someone said. The headline wrote itself.

By winter, the Watcher was gone. Crain sat behind bars, raving about butterscotch. The papers called him a lunatic. The department distanced itself. Rowen testified; people called her strange, not violent. The city decided Crain’s obsession had curdled into madness.

Only one man came out brighter: Martin Nelson. He became the gentle face at vigils, the steady back at doors, the one who spoke softly to grieving parents. He even shouldered Emma Darcy’s coffin when her father collapsed. Reporters praised him as the man who stayed at his post while the department unraveled.

One quiet evening, after yet another interview, Martin walked home. He whistled as he unlocked his basement. The door swung open.

From the dark, Leah Mercer raised her head.
